items.q: What does a telescope's aperture mainly determine?
items.correct: How much light it gathers and its resolving power.
items.why: A larger aperture collects more light and resolves finer detail, which matters more than magnification.

items.correct: A lens bending different colours by different amounts, blurring the image with colour fringes.
items.misconception: A mirror reflecting too much light.
items.why: Refraction varies with wavelength, so simple lenses focus colours at slightly different points.

items.q: Is magnification the most important property of a telescope?
items.correct: No - aperture (light-gathering and resolution) matters more than magnification.
items.misconception: Yes, more magnification always means a better telescope.
items.random_wrong: Magnification is the only thing that matters.
items.why: High magnification of a dim, blurry image is useless; aperture sets what you can actually see.

items.q: Chromatic aberration is mainly a problem of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Refractors. “Reflectors” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Resolving power improves when you increase:
items.why: Correct answer: Aperture. “Focal length only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Aperture of the Yerkes refractor in metres (to one decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.0. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
